extra.reasoning:
The only rule referencing vomiting is interaction_drop (priority 100, the highest of all rules): IF (back_pain and pallor and chest_pain and vision_loss and severe_headache and vomiting) THEN acuity := self_care. The monotonicity property requires that flipping vomiting from false to true (all else equal) never lowers acuity.

Counterexample comparison with back_pain=pallor=chest_pain=vision_loss=severe_headache=true, abdominal_pain=rash=false:

vomiting=FALSE: interaction_drop does not fire. Matching rules are raise_pallor (p3 -> emergent), raise_vision_loss (p3 -> emergent), and several p2 -> urgent rules (back_pain, severe_headache, chest_pain). Highest priority wins: the two p3 rules both set emergent. Result = emergent (the highest acuity).

vomiting=TRUE (the witness, all else unchanged): now all six conjuncts of interaction_drop are satisfied, so it fires. Its priority 100 exceeds every other matching rule (p3, p2), so it wins outright and sets acuity := self_care (the lowest acuity).

Setting vomiting true thus drops acuity from emergent to self_care, which is strictly lower in the listed order self_care < routine < urgent < emergent. This violates monotonicity. The witness lists the input state (vomiting=true) at which the property fails relative to its vomiting=false counterpart. abdominal_pain and rash are set false for a minimal witness; their values do not affect the outcome because interaction_drop (p100) dominates in the true case and the p3 emergent rules dominate in the false case regardless.
